pattern: Any winning substring can be shrunk to its minimum length without losing occurrences, so only the smallest window size needs counting under the unique-letter cap.
hints: If a longer substring is a frequent winner, the shortest allowed window sitting inside it occurs at least as often, so the larger sizes never help.
That means you only ever need to consider windows of exactly minSize and ignore maxSize entirely.
Slide a window of length minSize across the string, and whenever its distinct-letter count is within maxLetters, tally that exact window; the answer is the highest tally.

edge_inputs.description: overlapping windows of a single repeated letter
no window satisfies the unique-letter cap
window length equals the whole string
maxSize larger than needed must not change the count
a frequent long pattern but minSize windows decide the count

stress.description: long string over a small alphabet to maximise repeated windows
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    alphabet = 'abcde'
    s = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(n))
    maxLetters = rng.randint(1, 5)
    minSize = rng.randint(1, min(10, n))
    maxSize = rng.randint(minSize, min(26, n))
    return [s, maxLetters, minSize, maxSize]
stress.note: small alphabet forces many duplicate windows; sizes stay within string length
